binaries.title: dxgkrnl.sys - WIL Feature-Staging Migration of OPM (Output Protection Manager) Handles from Raw Kernel Pointers to Opaque IDs

changed_functions.name: VmBusOpmRequest (sub_1C0245C70)
key_changes: Removed a WIL feature-staging check (Feature_15692090__private_IsEnabledDeviceUsage, sub_1c0026850) that gated two VmBus command handling paths; one path calling CastToVmBusCommand<DXGKVMB_COMMAND_VSYNCREMOTINGCTRL> (sub_1c0040714) twice (once for validation, once for the operation) and the other calling sub_1c00405d4 once (no exact-header symbol resolved at 0x1c00405d4).
The unpatched version first checks Feature_15692090__private_IsEnabledDeviceUsage() and the cast result to decide whether to proceed; if the flag is off, it skips the operation entirely returning 0.
Patched version drops the flag branch and proceeds with the operation if the result is non-null.
changed_functions.security_notes: VmBus OPM request dispatch. The change is a WIL feature-staging (flighting) flag removal that commits to a single VmBus command handling path. No memory-safety consequence observed.

changed_functions.name: DXGSWAPCHAIN::DestroySurfacesResourcesLocal (sub_1C02ABA68)
key_changes: Removed a dual code path gated by WIL feature-staging flag Feature_342658361__private_IsEnabledDeviceUsage (sub_1c0027f20).

Flag ON (new, kept by the patch): takes an EXCLUSIVE handle-table lock via DXGHANDLETABLELOCKEXCLUSIVE::DXGHANDLETABLELOCKEXCLUSIVE (sub_1c0002630) / DXGAUTOPUSHLOCK::~DXGAUTOPUSHLOCK (sub_1c0004488), and while holding the lock sets the 0x2000 'in-destruction' bit on the handle-table entry before releasing.

Flag OFF (legacy, removed by the patch): takes only a SHARED push lock via DXGPUSHLOCK::AcquireShared (sub_1c0007018) + ExReleasePushLockSharedEx / KeLeaveCriticalRegion, and does NOT set the 0x2000 marking.
The patched version always uses the exclusive-lock + 0x2000-marking path.
changed_functions.security_notes: The legacy path (flag OFF) protects the handle-table lookup of the DXGSYNCOBJECT with only a SHARED push lock and does not set the 0x2000 'being destroyed' marker before releasing the lock. Because the DXGGLOBAL::DestroySyncObject call happens after the lock is released, two threads running the shared-lock path concurrently can both fetch the same object and both call DestroySyncObject on it, producing a double-free / use-after-free. The new path (flag ON, and the only path in the patched build) takes an EXCLUSIVE lock and sets the 0x2000 in-destruction bit while holding it, so a second thread sees (entry & 0x2000) != 0 and fetches nullptr. NOTE: this corrects the prior claim that the flag-ON path used the shared push lock and the flag-OFF path was lock-free; the mapping is reversed (ON = exclusive, OFF = shared).
changed_functions.assembly_diff:
UNPATCHED key section:
call    Feature_342658361__private_IsEnabledDeviceUsage  ; sub_1c0027f20
test    eax, eax
je      <legacy shared-lock path>
; NEW path (flag ON) - EXCLUSIVE handle-table lock:
lea     rcx, [rsp+...]
call    0x1c0002630       ; DXGHANDLETABLELOCKEXCLUSIVE::ctor (exclusive)
...lookup; on match set entry |= 0x2000...
call    0x1c0004488       ; DXGAUTOPUSHLOCK::dtor (release)
; LEGACY path (flag OFF) - SHARED push lock, no 0x2000 marking:
call    0x1c0007018       ; DXGPUSHLOCK::AcquireShared
...same lookup, no marking...
call    qword [rel ExReleasePushLockSharedEx]
call    qword [rel KeLeaveCriticalRegion]

PATCHED (always exclusive path):
call    0x1c0002630       ; DXGHANDLETABLELOCKEXCLUSIVE::ctor
...lookup; set entry |= 0x2000...
call    0x1c0004488       ; release

changed_functions.name: DXGSWAPCHAIN::DestroySurfacesResourcesGlobal (sub_1C02AB988)
key_changes: Removed WIL feature-staging checks (Feature_342658361__private_IsEnabledDeviceUsage, sub_1c0027f20) that gated whether DXGSYNCOBJECTLOCK::AcquireExclusive (sub_1c00186fc) was taken and whether the 4th argument to DXGGLOBAL::DestroySyncObject (sub_1c00ddb58) was 0 or 1.
Patched version always calls DXGSYNCOBJECTLOCK::AcquireExclusive and always passes arg4 = 1 to DestroySyncObject.
The arg4 value (0 vs 1) to DestroySyncObject controls teardown/assertion semantics inside DestroySyncObject.
changed_functions.security_notes: The legacy path (flag OFF) skips the exclusive DXGSYNCOBJECTLOCK before destroying the sync object and passes arg4=0. The patched build always acquires the exclusive sync-object lock and passes arg4=1, matching the locking hardening in DestroySurfacesResourcesLocal. (The prior report referenced the destroy function as sub_1c00dcb58; the actual destroy routine is DXGGLOBAL::DestroySyncObject at 0x1c00ddb58.)

changed_functions.name: ADAPTER_DISPLAY::OpmTranslateHandle (sub_1C0174F34)
key_changes: Removed a dual lookup path gated by WIL feature-staging flag Feature_2161340729__private_IsEnabledDeviceUsage (sub_1c00259e8).

Unpatched: flag ON uses ID-based lookup ADAPTER_DISPLAY::FindOpmAdapterMapping (result[3]==a2, the +0x18 ID field); flag OFF uses ADAPTER_DISPLAY::OpmValidateAdapterHandle (sub_1c0175044), which walks the list and returns 1 only if the raw node pointer a2 is an actual member.
Patched version always uses the ID-based FindOpmAdapterMapping (sub_1c00d6190, the same function relocated from 0x1c00d7190).
After a successful lookup/validation the function returns *(node + 0x10) via *a3.
changed_functions.security_notes: In the legacy (flag OFF) path the OPM handle IS the raw kernel address of the mapping node; OpmValidateAdapterHandle re-validates it by walking the adapter's OPM list and requires an exact pointer match (i == a2) before use. This is NOT a type confusion or arbitrary read: an attacker-supplied value that is not a live member returns 0 (error), and a value that is a live member is the legitimate node. The real weakness of the legacy scheme is that a raw kernel pointer is used as (and handed back as) the OPM handle, which discloses a kernel address to the caller and weakens KASLR. The patched build migrates to opaque 64-bit IDs (FindOpmAdapterMapping), removing the kernel-pointer-as-handle exposure.

changed_functions.name: ADAPTER_DISPLAY::OpmTranslateAndDestroyHandle (sub_1C0174AA8)
key_changes: Same dual-path removal as OpmTranslateHandle: removed WIL feature-staging flag Feature_2161340729__private_IsEnabledDeviceUsage (sub_1c00259e8) gating between ID-based lookup (FindOpmAdapterMapping) when ON and pointer-membership validation (OpmValidateAdapterHandle, sub_1c0175044) when OFF.
After lookup/validation this function unlinks the node from the doubly-linked list and frees it with operator delete (sub_1c0002824).
The unlink includes a __fastfail(3) consistency check verifying flink->blink == node and blink->flink == node before writing the two neighbor pointers.
Patched version always uses ID-based lookup (sub_1c00d6190) and takes a 64-bit ID (a2 is now __int64, not a pointer).
changed_functions.security_notes: The unlink writes (*blink = flink; *(flink+8) = blink) but only after either (a) FindOpmAdapterMapping returned a real node by ID, or (b) OpmValidateAdapterHandle confirmed the pointer is a live list member, AND a __fastfail integrity check on the neighbor links passes. There is therefore NO write-what-where / arbitrary-write primitive: the node being unlinked is always a genuine, consistency-checked member of this adapter's list. The security-relevant aspect is the same as OpmTranslateHandle: the legacy path uses raw kernel pointers as OPM handles (kernel-address disclosure), and the patch migrates to opaque IDs. (This corrects the prior claim of a pointer-vs-ID type confusion yielding a controlled kernel write.)

changed_functions.name: ADAPTER_DISPLAY::OpmCreateHandle (sub_1C0175174)
key_changes: Removed a dual path gated by WIL feature-staging flag Feature_2161340729__private_IsEnabledDeviceUsage (sub_1c00259e8).
Flag OFF (legacy): inserts the new mapping node at the list head (this+0x130) WITHOUT generating an ID, and returns the raw node pointer to the caller (*a3 = node).
Flag ON (new / patched): generates a unique non-zero 64-bit ID via a loop incrementing *(this+0x140), stores it at node+0x18, inserts the node, and returns the ID to the caller (*a3 = id).
changed_functions.security_notes: This is the allocation counterpart to the translate/destroy functions. In the legacy path the OPM handle value returned to the caller is the raw kernel pointer of the freshly allocated _OPM_HANDLE_MAPPING node (kernel-address disclosure to the OPM client). The new path returns an opaque, uniquely generated 64-bit ID and stamps it into the node's +0x18 field so later ID-based lookups succeed. The patch removes the pointer-returning legacy path, eliminating the kernel-pointer-as-handle exposure.

changed_functions.name: DXGGLOBAL::DestroySyncObject (sub_1C00DDB58)
key_changes: Removed a WIL feature-staging flag (Feature_342658361__private_IsEnabledDeviceUsage, sub_1c0027f20) that gated internal assertion logic. The unpatched version has two assertion paths with different WdAssertion codes (0xac7, 0xacb, 0xad0 vs patched 0xac6, 0xaca).
The core destruction logic is otherwise identical.
changed_functions.security_notes: Assertion/diagnostic code shift tied to the same feature-staging removal. No behavioral change to the destruction path itself.

changed_functions.name: DXG_HOST_VIRTUALGPU_VMBUS::VmBusGetRegistryKeys (sub_1C02430A0)
key_changes: Most changes are call-target address shifts due to binary layout differences (e.g., sub_1c0040854 -> sub_1c0040714, sub_1c003f26c -> sub_1c003f12c).
One logic change: 'if (!<feature-flag>() || rax_5)' changed to 'if (rax_5)' - removing a WIL feature-staging check.
Registry key enumeration logic is otherwise identical.

changed_functions.name: VIDPN_MGR::BuildSetTimingsPathInfoFromClientVidPn (sub_1C0142590)
key_changes: Predominantly call-target address shifts due to binary layout differences.
Large function (1196 instructions, 211 basic blocks) with minimal structural changes - mainly call target addresses shifted.

security_findings.entry_point: OPM (Output Protection Manager) IOCTL dispatch: DpiPdoDispatchInternalIoctl (sub_1c016a270) -> DpiPdoHandleOpmIoctls (sub_1c0174bd4) -> DxgkOpmTranslateAndDestroyHandle (sub_1c0174a00) / DxgkOpmTranslateHandle (sub_1c0174e8c) / DxgkOpmCreateHandle -> ADAPTER_DISPLAY::Opm* handlers
security_findings.vulnerability_class: Information Exposure (kernel address disclosure) via raw-kernel-pointer-as-handle; hardened to opaque IDs. NOT type confusion / arbitrary write.
security_findings.description: The unpatched dxgkrnl.sys carries a WIL feature-staging (flighting) flag Feature_2161340729__private_IsEnabledDeviceUsage (sub_1c00259e8) that selects between two OPM handle schemes in ADAPTER_DISPLAY. In the legacy scheme (flag OFF), ADAPTER_DISPLAY::OpmCreateHandle returns the raw kernel address of the newly allocated _OPM_HANDLE_MAPPING node as the OPM handle handed back to the caller, and ADAPTER_DISPLAY::OpmTranslateHandle / OpmTranslateAndDestroyHandle re-validate a supplied handle by walking the adapter's OPM list with ADAPTER_DISPLAY::OpmValidateAdapterHandle (sub_1c0175044), which returns 1 only when the raw pointer exactly matches a live list member (i == a2). In the new scheme (flag ON, and the only scheme in the patched build), the handle is an opaque uniquely generated 64-bit ID stored at node+0x18, and lookups use ADAPTER_DISPLAY::FindOpmAdapterMapping (sub_1c00d7190 unpatched / sub_1c00d6190 patched; the SAME function, relocated) comparing result[3] == a2. The patch removes the legacy pointer-handle path. The security benefit is eliminating disclosure of a kernel heap address to the OPM client (a KASLR-weakening info leak) and the fragile pointer-as-handle pattern. It is NOT a memory-corruption bug: the destroy path validates list membership and includes a __fastfail(3) neighbor-link consistency check before unlinking, so a mismatched or attacker-chosen non-member value takes the error path and never triggers a wrong-object write.
security_findings.trigger_conditions:
1. The WIL feature-staging flag Feature_2161340729__private_IsEnabledDeviceUsage (sub_1c00259e8, reading the WIL feature global) must be in the OFF state, selecting the legacy pointer-handle scheme.
2. Issue OPM IOCTLs to the DirectX graphics kernel that reach OpmCreateHandle (to obtain a handle) and OpmTranslateHandle / OpmTranslateAndDestroyHandle (to translate/destroy).
3. Observe that the handle value returned by OpmCreateHandle is a kernel pointer (0xFFFF...) rather than a small opaque counter, disclosing a kernel address.
security_findings.primitive: Kernel-address information disclosure: the OPM handle returned to the caller in the legacy path is the kernel address of the _OPM_HANDLE_MAPPING node, usable to defeat KASLR for the OPM handle pool. No write-what-where or arbitrary-read primitive exists: OpmValidateAdapterHandle requires an exact list-member pointer match and the unlink is guarded by a __fastfail consistency check, so only genuine, validated nodes are ever translated or unlinked.
security_findings.assembly:
; UNPATCHED ADAPTER_DISPLAY::OpmValidateAdapterHandle (sub_1c0175044) - pointer-membership validation, returns bool
; rbx = this, rdi = candidate handle (raw pointer in legacy scheme)
0x1c017509a: lea rcx, [rbx+0x130]     ; rcx = &list head (this+0x130)
0x1c01750a1: mov rax, [rcx]           ; rax = first list node
0x1c01750a4: cmp rax, rcx             ; wrapped back to head? (end of list)
0x1c01750a7: jz  0x1c01750c1          ; end -> return 0
0x1c01750a9: cmp rax, rdi             ; exact NODE-POINTER match against a live member
0x1c01750ac: jnz 0x1c01750bc          ; not this node -> advance
0x1c01750ae: mov al, 1               ; genuine list node -> return 1
0x1c01750bc: mov rax, [rax]           ; next node
0x1c01750bf: jmp 0x1c01750a4
0x1c01750c1: xor al, al               ; non-member -> return 0

; FindOpmAdapterMapping - ID lookup (same routine in both builds; addresses for PATCHED sub_1c00d6190, unpatched sub_1c00d7190)
; rbx = this, rdi = candidate ID
0x1c00d6201: lea rcx, [rbx+0x130]     ; rcx = &list head (this+0x130)
0x1c00d6208: mov rax, [rcx]           ; rax = first list node
0x1c00d620b: jmp 0x1c00d6216
0x1c00d620d: cmp [rax+0x18], rdi      ; +0x18 ID field comparison
0x1c00d6211: jz  0x1c00d621d          ; match -> return node
0x1c00d6213: mov rax, [rax]           ; next node
0x1c00d6216: cmp rax, rcx             ; wrapped back to head? (end of list)
0x1c00d6219: jnz 0x1c00d620d          ; more nodes -> keep scanning
0x1c00d621b: xor eax, eax             ; not found -> nullptr

breakpoints.why: In the legacy (flag OFF) path this writes *a3 = raw node pointer. Step to the '*a3 = v11' store and inspect the value handed back to the OPM client; a kernel pointer here confirms the info-disclosure scheme.

The legacy pointer-membership validation (returns bool). When hit, rcx = adapter (this), rdx = candidate handle. Confirm the loop compares rax (list node) to rdx and only returns 1 on an exact member match - i.e., there is no wrong-object match for a non-member value.

WIL feature-staging flag that selects the OPM handle scheme. Return value 0 (eax==0) selects the legacy raw-pointer-handle path. NOTE: in the PATCHED binary address 0x1c00259e8 is a different function (DXGOVERLAYMUTEX constructor) due to address reuse; this breakpoint is meaningful only in the unpatched image.

watch: *a3 after OpmCreateHandle legacy path - if it holds a kernel pointer (0xFFFF...), the raw-pointer-handle info-leak scheme is active
eax after sub_1c00259e8 returns - 0 selects the legacy pointer-handle path
rdx into OpmValidateAdapterHandle - the candidate handle; a non-member value makes the loop fall through to return 0 (error), NOT a wrong-object match
node+0x18 (result[3]) - the opaque ID field used by the new/patched FindOpmAdapterMapping lookup
key_offsets: 0x1c01750a9: 'cmp rax, rdi' in OpmValidateAdapterHandle - exact list-member pointer check against the candidate handle (legacy validation)
node+0x00 = Flink, node+0x08 = Blink, node+0x10 = OPM object returned via *a3, node+0x18 = opaque ID
debugging_aids.trigger_setup:
1. Open a handle to the DirectX graphics kernel device and drive the OPM protected-output APIs
2. Call the OPM create path (DxgkOpmCreateHandle -> OpmCreateHandle) to obtain an OPM handle
3. With the feature flag OFF, observe the returned handle is the kernel address of the mapping node (KASLR-weakening disclosure)
4. Use OpmTranslateHandle / OpmTranslateAndDestroyHandle with that handle; the pointer is re-validated by list-membership walk before use
debugging_aids.expected_observation: In the legacy path, OpmCreateHandle hands back a kernel pointer as the OPM handle; OpmValidateAdapterHandle confirms membership on translate/destroy. Supplying a non-member value simply returns error status 0xC01E050C (no crash, no corruption). No BSOD is expected from this path under normal validation; the only security consequence is kernel-address disclosure.
debugging_aids.notes: The dual scheme is controlled by WIL feature-staging flags (Feature_<id>__private_IsEnabledDeviceUsage), a staged-rollout mechanism. Struct layout: _OPM_HANDLE_MAPPING +0x00 Flink, +0x08 Blink, +0x10 OPM object (returned to caller), +0x18 opaque ID. FindOpmAdapterMapping is the SAME function in both builds (0x1c00d7190 unpatched, 0x1c00d6190 patched) - a relocation, not a fix. The patch's fix is removing the legacy pointer-handle path so OPM handles are always opaque IDs.

security_findings.entry_point: Swapchain surface teardown reached via swapchain destruction paths (DXGSWAPCHAIN::DestroySwapchain* )
security_findings.vulnerability_class: Race Condition (insufficient lock strength) leading to double-free / Use-After-Free
security_findings.description: The unpatched DXGSWAPCHAIN::DestroySurfacesResourcesLocal (sub_1c02aba68) has two code paths gated by WIL feature-staging flag Feature_342658361__private_IsEnabledDeviceUsage (sub_1c0027f20). The legacy path (flag OFF) protects the handle-table lookup of the swapchain's DXGSYNCOBJECT with only a SHARED push lock (DXGPUSHLOCK::AcquireShared, sub_1c0007018) and does NOT set the 0x2000 'in-destruction' marker on the handle-table entry. Because DXGGLOBAL::DestroySyncObject is invoked after the lock is released, two threads executing the shared-lock path concurrently can both fetch the same DXGSYNCOBJECT and both destroy it, producing a double-free / use-after-free. The new path (flag ON, and the only path in the patched build) takes an EXCLUSIVE handle-table lock (DXGHANDLETABLELOCKEXCLUSIVE, sub_1c0002630 / sub_1c0004488) and sets the 0x2000 bit while holding it, so a concurrent thread sees the marker and fetches nullptr. The sibling function DestroySurfacesResourcesGlobal (sub_1c02ab988) mirrors this: the legacy path skips the exclusive DXGSYNCOBJECTLOCK (sub_1c00186fc) and passes arg4=0 to DestroySyncObject, while the patched path always acquires the exclusive lock and passes arg4=1.
security_findings.trigger_conditions:
1. Feature flag Feature_342658361__private_IsEnabledDeviceUsage (sub_1c0027f20) is OFF, selecting the shared-lock path with no 0x2000 marking.
2. Two concurrent swapchain-destroy operations reach DestroySurfacesResourcesLocal targeting the same swapchain surface / sync-object handle.
3. The race window is between the shared-locked lookup (which does not mark the entry) and the post-lock DXGGLOBAL::DestroySyncObject call.
security_findings.primitive: Double-free / use-after-free of a DXGSYNCOBJECT: two concurrent shared-lock-path threads both resolve and destroy the same object. The freed pool allocation can be reclaimed with attacker-controlled data before or after the second free, enabling further corruption.
security_findings.assembly:
; UNPATCHED DXGSWAPCHAIN::DestroySurfacesResourcesLocal (sub_1c02aba68)
call    Feature_342658361__private_IsEnabledDeviceUsage  ; sub_1c0027f20
test    eax, eax
je      <legacy shared-lock path>
; NEW path (flag ON): EXCLUSIVE lock + 0x2000 marking
call    0x1c0002630       ; DXGHANDLETABLELOCKEXCLUSIVE::ctor
...or entry, 0x2000...
call    0x1c0004488       ; DXGAUTOPUSHLOCK::dtor
jmp     <destroy>
; LEGACY path (flag OFF): SHARED lock, NO marking
lea     rcx, [current+0xd0]
call    0x1c0007018       ; DXGPUSHLOCK::AcquireShared
...lookup...
call    qword [rel ExReleasePushLockSharedEx]
call    qword [rel KeLeaveCriticalRegion]
<destroy>:
call    0x1c00ddb58       ; DXGGLOBAL::DestroySyncObject (after lock released)

; PATCHED (single exclusive path)
call    0x1c0002630       ; DXGHANDLETABLELOCKEXCLUSIVE::ctor
...lookup; or entry,0x2000...
call    0x1c0004488       ; DXGAUTOPUSHLOCK::dtor

breakpoints.why: Break at entry, then step past the sub_1c0027f20 call. eax==0 selects the legacy SHARED-lock path (racy, no 0x2000 marking); eax!=0 selects the EXCLUSIVE-lock path that marks the entry.
WIL feature-staging flag selecting the lock strength. eax==0 => legacy shared lock (double-destroy possible); eax!=0 => exclusive lock + 0x2000 marking.

watch: eax after sub_1c0027f20 - 0 selects the shared-lock (racy) path
handle-table entry dword (the one that receives |= 0x2000) - only the exclusive path sets it; absence of the marker is what allows the double-destroy
the DXGSYNCOBJECT pointer (v14/v15) fetched from the table and passed to DestroySyncObject
concurrent second-thread fetch of the same table index
key_offsets: call DXGPUSHLOCK::AcquireShared (sub_1c0007018) - shared lock, legacy path only
call DXGHANDLETABLELOCKEXCLUSIVE::ctor (sub_1c0002630) - exclusive lock, new/patched path
'entry |= 0x2000' store - in-destruction marker, new/patched path only
call DXGGLOBAL::DestroySyncObject (sub_1c00ddb58) - executed after the lock is released
debugging_aids.trigger_setup:
1. Create a swapchain with sync objects via D3DKMT swapchain APIs
2. Issue concurrent swapchain / surface destruction from multiple threads targeting the same surface
3. With the feature flag OFF, the shared-lock path lets two threads both resolve and destroy the same DXGSYNCOBJECT
debugging_aids.expected_observation: BSOD 0x19 (BAD_POOL_HEADER) or 0xC2 (BAD_POOL_CALLER) from double-free of the DXGSYNCOBJECT pool block, or a delayed use-after-free crash if the block is reclaimed between the two frees. The patched exclusive-lock + 0x2000-marking path serializes and de-duplicates the destroy, preventing the second free.
debugging_aids.notes: The fix standardizes on the exclusive handle-table lock plus the 0x2000 in-destruction marker (the new WIL-staged path), removing the legacy shared-lock path. IMPORTANT correction over the prior report: the flag-ON path is the EXCLUSIVE lock (safe), and the flag-OFF path is the SHARED push lock (racy) - the earlier report reversed this mapping and mislabeled these swapchain-surface functions as generic 'sync object table cleanup'.

summary: This patch is a WIL feature-staging (flighting) cleanup, not a memory-corruption fix on the primary path. The unpatched dxgkrnl.sys carries feature-staging flags Feature_2161340729__private_IsEnabledDeviceUsage (sub_1c00259e8) and Feature_342658361__private_IsEnabledDeviceUsage (sub_1c0027f20) that gate legacy vs. new implementations of (1) OPM (Output Protection Manager) handle management in ADAPTER_DISPLAY and (2) swapchain sync-object teardown in DXGSWAPCHAIN. For OPM, the legacy scheme (flag OFF) uses the raw kernel address of the _OPM_HANDLE_MAPPING node as the OPM handle handed back to the caller (a kernel-address / KASLR-weakening information disclosure) and re-validates it via ADAPTER_DISPLAY::OpmValidateAdapterHandle (sub_1c0175044), an exact list-membership check; the new scheme uses opaque 64-bit IDs looked up by ADAPTER_DISPLAY::FindOpmAdapterMapping (sub_1c00d7190 unpatched / sub_1c00d6190 patched - the same function relocated). There is NO type-confusion or write-what-where primitive here: the destroy path only unlinks a validated, __fastfail-consistency-checked member. The genuinely memory-safety-relevant change is in DXGSWAPCHAIN::DestroySurfacesResourcesLocal/Global (sub_1c02aba68 / sub_1c02ab988): the legacy path takes only a SHARED push lock and omits the 0x2000 in-destruction marker, so two concurrent swapchain-destroy threads can double-free the same DXGSYNCOBJECT (use-after-free); the patched build always takes the EXCLUSIVE handle-table lock and sets the marker. The patch removes all these feature-staging branches, committing to the opaque-ID OPM handles and the exclusive-lock swapchain teardown.
